race.name: Cape Town Cycle Tour
race.tagline: 35,000 riders. Chapman's Peak. The entire Cape Peninsula in a single day. The world's largest individually timed cycling event.

vitals.location: Cape Town, Western Cape, South Africa
vitals.start_time: Seeded waves from 6:00 AM based on qualifying times; elite riders depart first, recreational groups follow in staggered batches through mid-morning
vitals.registration: Online via capetowncycletour.com. International entry: R1,700 (~$93 USD). Local entries from R700 (PPA members) to R940 (general). 42 km route: R500. Opens mid-year prior; entries close end of January.
vitals.prize_purse: None (timed mass-participation event with age-group results)
vitals.aid_stations: 12 refreshment stations on the 109 km route with water, energy drinks, bananas, and energy bars. Medical teams, physiotherapists, and repair zones at key stations. Major support at Chapman's Peak summit and Hout Bay.
vitals.cutoff_time: 7 hours from start wave departure (109 km). Cutoff point at Chapman's Peak (16:00).
distance_options: 109 km (67.7 mi) — the full Cape Peninsula circuit, flagship event
78 km — shortened route bypassing some Peninsula sections (used for elite women's race in 2025)
42 km — Constantia/Newlands loop launched in 2023 for newer cyclists

climate.primary: Late Southern Hemisphere summer — warm, dry, and potentially windy
climate.description: March in Cape Town marks the tail end of summer. Temperatures typically range from 15-27C (59-81F) with daytime averages around 24C (75F). The month averages only two rainy days and roughly 293 hours of sunshine, making conditions generally excellent for cycling. The dominant hazard is the Southeast 'Cape Doctor' wind, which funnels through Chapman's Peak and along the Atlantic Seaboard. In extreme years, gusts have exceeded 100 km/h and caused event cancellations. The UV intensity is high — six hours of Southern Hemisphere sun with limited shade demands serious sun protection.
challenges: Southeast 'Cape Doctor' wind: gusts of 40-100 km/h are possible — event has been cancelled (2017) and stopped (2009) due to extreme wind
Sun exposure: 6+ hours of intense Southern Hemisphere UV with limited shade on the Peninsula
Heat on sheltered sections: 25C+ in windless valleys while wind chill bites on exposed coast
Sudden wind direction changes around Chapman's Peak and along the Atlantic coast

terrain.primary: Paved coastal roads circling the Cape Peninsula
terrain.surface: Well-maintained South African national and metropolitan roads. All tarmac throughout. No unpaved sections. Road surfaces are excellent on the main route, periodically resurfaced for the event.
features: Chapman's Peak Drive — iconic 9 km coastal cliff road carved into the mountain above the Atlantic, with 114 curves. Two-part climb: 'Little Chappies' is short and steep, followed by the longer main ascent averaging 3-5%.
Suikerbossie Hill — the sting in the tail at km 95. 2.8 km from Hout Bay roundabout to summit, averaging 4.4% with a brutal kilometre-and-a-half of sustained climbing when legs are spent.
Edinburgh Drive (Wynberg Hill) — short sharp early climb that wakes up the legs
False Bay coastline — long exposed sections along the Indian Ocean coast through Simon's Town and Scarborough
Atlantic Seaboard — Camps Bay and Sea Point sections with Table Mountain and the Twelve Apostles as backdrop

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: The world's largest individually timed cycling event (28,000-35,000 riders). 48th edition in 2026. UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier from 2026. Chapman's Peak is one of cycling's great visual experiences. South Africa's biggest one-day sporting event and a Cape Town civic institution.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: adventure=5 and experience=5 reflect one of the most spectacular cycling routes on Earth — the full Cape Peninsula circuit with Table Mountain, Chapman's Peak, and two oceans. length=2 and elevation=2 because 109 km with 1,220m climbing is moderate by global standards. value=5 for $38-93 entry to an event of this scale. cultural_impact=4 for deep South African sporting significance, Cape Town civic institution status, 46-year history, and growing international profile with UCI Gran Fondo World Series status. prestige=4 with tier promotion blocked from T1 per p4 rules.

logistics.explanation: Slick operation refined over 47 editions. Seeded start waves manage 28,000-35,000 riders with remarkably little chaos. Twelve refreshment stations, medical support, and marshalling are comprehensive. Cape Town International Airport (CPT) is 25-30 minutes from the city center — far easier access than most European mountain events. The Expo at the Cape Town International Convention Centre is well-organized. The only friction: international riders need to navigate South African traffic driving on the left, the sheer scale means packet pickup queues can be substantial, and if connecting through Johannesburg you must collect and re-check bags.

length.explanation: 109 km is a solid day out but not a long one by global cycling event standards. Most fit riders finish in 3.5-5 hours. The 7-hour cutoff is generous. This is an event that rewards speed over endurance — it's about how fast you can go, not whether you can survive. The distance is deliberately accessible: the Cape Town Cycle Tour wants 35,000 riders, not 3,500.

technicality.explanation: All paved roads. The Chapman's Peak descent requires basic bike handling on a winding road with 114 curves, but the technical demands are minimal. The primary challenge is wind management — staying aero in crosswinds, sheltering in groups, and handling gusts on exposed coastal sections. You do not need anything beyond basic road cycling skills.

elevation.explanation: 1,220 meters of climbing over 109 km is rolling, not mountainous. Chapman's Peak (~450m) is the only sustained climb, and it's over in 20-30 minutes. Suikerbossie Hill (2.8 km at 4.4%) at km 95 is sharp but brief. Edinburgh Drive is an early wake-up call. This is not a climbing event. The elevation score reflects a course that has character and undulation without ever approaching Alpine or Andean severity.

climate.explanation: March in Cape Town is generally benign — warm, dry, sunny, with only two average rain days in the month. The only real hazard is the Southeast wind, and when it blows hard, it dominates the entire experience. In most years, conditions are near-perfect for cycling. The UV is intense but manageable with sunscreen. Compared to Alpine cold, desert heat, or tropical humidity, Cape Town's climate is among the friendliest on the global cycling calendar.

altitude.explanation: The route maxes out around 600m on Chapman's Peak. There are zero altitude effects. Sea-level riders have no adaptation concerns whatsoever.

adventure.explanation: This is where the Cape Town Cycle Tour earns its keep. The route circles the entire Cape Peninsula — one of the most dramatic landscapes on Earth. Table Mountain towers over the start. Chapman's Peak Drive is a cliff road carved into the mountainside with the Atlantic Ocean 200m below. You ride past Cape Point, along the Indian Ocean coast, through fishing villages, around the Peninsula's wild southern tip, and finish with the Atlantic Seaboard's Camps Bay and Sea Point. The scenery is genuinely world-class. There is no cycling event on Earth with a more spectacular urban-coastal route.

prestige.explanation: The world's largest individually timed cycling event. Running since 1978. Born from a protest ride advocating for cycling infrastructure. The 2026 addition to the UCI Gran Fondo World Series elevates the event from a mass-participation ride to a qualifier for World Championships — a significant prestige boost. Chapman's Peak is iconic in South African culture. The event lacks the deep European cycling mythology of L'Etape or Maratona dles Dolomites, which keeps it from a 5, but within the Southern Hemisphere and the global mass-participation cycling world, this is royalty.

race_quality.explanation: Individually timed with seeded start waves based on qualifying times. Chip timing, age-group results, and now UCI Gran Fondo qualification create genuine competitive structure. The organization manages 28,000-35,000 riders on fully or mostly closed roads with medical support and marshalling at a scale few events in the world attempt. The 2025 edition produced record-breaking times in near-perfect conditions, demonstrating the course's capacity for genuine racing. The seeding system means fast riders get clean roads and the experience improves at every level.

experience.explanation: Chapman's Peak. That is the experience. Climbing the cliff road with the Atlantic Ocean spread out below you, Table Mountain behind you, and tens of thousands of other riders sharing the moment. The descent into Hout Bay with the harbor below. The finish along the Atlantic Seaboard with Camps Bay beach on one side and the Twelve Apostles mountain range on the other. Add the energy of the world's largest individually timed field and the experience is unforgettable. This is not a race you do for the competition. It is a race you do because Chapman's Peak on a closed road is one of cycling's great moments.

community.explanation: 28,000-35,000 riders. That number alone creates a community event unlike almost anything else in cycling. The Cape Town Cycle Tour is a civic institution — it shuts down an entire city. Schools close. Families line the route. The mix of serious racers, weekend riders, charity fundraisers, and first-timers creates an atmosphere closer to a marathon than a typical cycling event. In 2024, over 10,500 riders came from outside the Western Cape representing 46 countries. But this is fundamentally a Cape Town event, and the city owns it.

field_depth.explanation: The sharp end features South Africa's top amateur and semi-pro riders, plus visiting international competitors chasing UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualification. Tyler Lange's 2025 course record (2:25:48) and Pia Grunewald's astonishing solo breakaway (2:05:17 on the 78 km women's course) demonstrate serious racing at the front. But 95% of the field is recreational — families, charity riders, club cyclists out for a great day on the Peninsula. The depth is wide rather than deep.

value.explanation: R700-R1,700 ($38-93 USD) for the world's largest individually timed cycling event on one of Earth's most spectacular cycling routes. With chip timing, seeded start waves, 12 refreshment stations, medical support, road closures for 109 km, and now UCI Gran Fondo qualification — this is absurdly good value. South African pricing makes this one of the most affordable major cycling events in the world. Even international entry at $93 is a fraction of what comparable European events charge.

expenses.explanation: Cape Town is affordable by Western standards but requires a long-haul flight for most international riders. Return flights from the US run $800-1,500, from Europe $500-900. Accommodation ranges from budget hostels ($30/night) to boutique hotels ($150+/night) — March is shoulder season so rates are reasonable. The favorable exchange rate (approximately 18 ZAR to 1 USD) makes food, transport, and activities cheap once you arrive. The event generated an estimated R500 million in economic impact in 2024, reflecting how many visitors combine it with a Cape Town holiday. Budget $1,500-3,000 total from the US including flights and a week's stay.

biased_opinion.overview:
The Cape Town Cycle Tour is not the hardest cycling event you will ever do. At 109 km with 1,220m of climbing, it is shorter and flatter than dozens of European sportives. What it is, unambiguously, is the most spectacular urban cycling event on Earth. The route circles the entire Cape Peninsula — Table Mountain, Chapman's Peak, Cape Point, two oceans, fishing villages, and the Atlantic Seaboard — on closed roads with tens of thousands of other riders. Chapman's Peak Drive, a cliff road carved into the mountainside 200 meters above the Atlantic, is the single most visually stunning segment of any mass-participation cycling event anywhere.

Born from a 1977 protest ride advocating for cycling infrastructure — when Bill Mylrea and John Stegmann organized the 'Big Ride In' — it has grown from 525 riders in 1978 to the world's largest individually timed event and a Cape Town civic institution. The 2026 addition to the UCI Gran Fondo World Series adds competitive teeth to what was already a bucket-list ride. Tyler Lange shattered the 17-year-old course record in 2025 with a 2:25:48 finish, while Pia Grunewald's 50 km solo breakaway in the women's race demonstrated that serious racing lives at the sharp end of this massive field.

The Southeast 'Cape Doctor' wind is the variable you cannot control — it has cancelled the event outright (2017), stopped it mid-race (2009), and shortened it to 47 km (2015 fire). But when conditions cooperate, and the Cape Peninsula delivers one of its flawless March days, there is simply nothing else like it in cycling. You do not come here for suffering. You come here for Chapman's Peak.

final_verdict.one_liner: The world's largest individually timed cycling event. 35,000 riders. Chapman's Peak. The entire Cape Peninsula on closed roads.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want the most spectacular coastal cycling route on Earth and the energy of 28,000-35,000 riders sharing closed roads around the Cape Peninsula. Yes if you want UCI Gran Fondo World Championship qualification in a stunning setting at an unbeatable price point. Skip if you need serious climbing or endurance challenge — 109 km with 1,220m of climbing is moderate. Skip if the Southeast wind gamble (potential event cancellation) is a dealbreaker for a long-haul trip.
alternatives: 947 Ride Joburg — South Africa's other mega-ride, 94.7 km through Johannesburg with 30,000+ riders
Tour Durban — 106 km coastal sportive in KwaZulu-Natal for another South African road event
L'Etape du Tour — if you want a massive European timed sportive with Tour de France mythology
Ford RideLondon — was the Northern Hemisphere's equivalent (25,000 riders, closed London roads) before its indefinite pause

logistics.nearest_airport: Cape Town International Airport (CPT) — 25-30 minutes from the city center. Direct flights from major European and Middle Eastern hubs (London, Amsterdam, Frankfurt, Doha, Dubai). US connections via Johannesburg (JNB) or Middle Eastern hubs. Note: if connecting through Johannesburg, you must collect and re-check bags even if tagged through to Cape Town.
logistics.travel_tips: Cape Town drives on the left — take care if renting a car. The MyCiTi bus system connects the airport to the city center and Sea Point. Uber and Bolt are widely available and affordable. March is shoulder season with pleasant weather and fewer tourists than December-January. Most international riders combine the event with a week of Cape Town tourism (Table Mountain, Cape Winelands, Cape Point). The favorable exchange rate (~18 ZAR to 1 USD) makes Cape Town excellent value once you arrive. Packet pickup is at the Cape Town International Convention Centre 2-3 days before the event — go early on day one to beat the crowds.
logistics.accommodation: Stay in the Cape Town city center (close to the Grand Parade start), Sea Point or Green Point (close to the finish), or Camps Bay (on the route). March is shoulder season so rates are reasonable. Book 3-6 months ahead for event weekend — the event generates R500 million in economic activity and accommodation fills quickly. Budget hostels from $30/night, mid-range hotels $80-150/night, boutique/luxury options $150-400/night. Many international riders use Airbnb.
logistics.gear_recommendations: Road bike with standard gearing — the climbing is moderate and a compact crankset (50/34) with an 11-28 cassette is more than sufficient. Sunscreen is non-negotiable: Southern Hemisphere UV in March is intense. Arm warmers for the cool early morning start (6 AM waves). Lightweight wind vest for the exposed coastal sections — if the Southeast wind blows, you will want protection. Sunglasses that work in both full sun and the shaded Chapman's Peak tunnels. Two water bottles minimum; 12 refreshment stations are well-spaced but the exposed sections can be dehydrating.

history.first_edition: 1978 (originated from the 1977 'Big Ride In' protest ride)
notable_editions: 1977: The 'Big Ride In' protest ride organized by Bill Mylrea and John Stegmann to advocate for cycling infrastructure in Cape Town — hundreds of cyclists participated including the Mayor of Cape Town
1978: First official Cape Argus Pick n Pay Cycle Tour — 525 riders. Won by Lawrence Whittaker in September 1978
1993: Wimpie van der Merwe set the all-time fastest time (2:16:40, averaging 46.1 km/h) on a fully faired recumbent on the 105 km course
2002: Race stopped at Ou Kaapse Weg when temperatures reached 42C (108F) — extreme heat cancellation
2003: Reached 35,000 riders, cementing status as the world's largest individually timed cycling event
2008: Robbie Hunter set the men's standard bicycle record at 2:27:29 — stood for 17 years
2009: Race stopped at Chapman's Peak due to 100 km/h wind gusts that blew cyclists off their bikes
2014: Renamed from Cape Argus Pick n Pay Momentum Cycle Tour to Cape Town Cycle Tour
2015: Route drastically shortened to 47 km due to a major wildfire closing Chapman's Peak Drive
2017: Cancelled on race day — wind speeds exceeding 100 km/h plus fires and protest threats forced organizers to halt after the first wave had started
2020: Held in March just before COVID-19 lockdowns
2021: Postponed from March to October due to COVID-19 second wave; reduced to 18,000 entries with modified protocols

2025: Tyler Lange smashed the men's record with 2:25:48 (beating Hunter's 17-year-old mark by 1:41). Pia Grunewald shattered the women's record with a 50 km solo breakaway, finishing in 2:05:17 on the 78 km course
2026: Joins the UCI Gran Fondo World Series — age-group qualification for World Championships
history.records: Men's course record: Tyler Lange, 2:25:48 (2025, 109 km). Previous: Robbie Hunter, 2:27:29 (2008). Women's course record: Pia Grunewald, 2:05:17 (2025, 78 km course). Previous: Cherise Willeit (2020). All-time fastest: Wimpie van der Merwe, 2:16:40 on a fully faired recumbent (1993, 105 km course).
history.growth: From 525 riders in 1978 to 35,000+ by the early 2000s. The 2025 edition drew approximately 28,000 entrants from 46 countries. The 2024 event attracted over 10,500 riders from outside the Western Cape. The event generates an estimated R500 million ($28 million USD) in economic impact annually. The first cycling event outside Europe included in the UCI Gran Fondo World Series (2026).

course_description.overview: A 109 km clockwise circuit of the Cape Peninsula starting from the Grand Parade in central Cape Town and finishing at the Green Point Cricket Fields. The route heads south through the Southern Suburbs, picks up the Indian Ocean coastline at Muizenberg, follows False Bay through Kalk Bay, Simon's Town, and Fish Hoek to Scarborough, then crosses the Peninsula to the Atlantic side via the iconic Chapman's Peak Drive. After descending to Hout Bay, riders tackle the final sting of Suikerbossie Hill before the Atlantic Seaboard run through Camps Bay and Sea Point to the finish. Total elevation gain approximately 1,220m. The route is designed to showcase the Cape Peninsula's extraordinary geography while remaining accessible to 28,000-35,000 riders.

segments.name: Grand Parade to Muizenberg
segments.description: From the Grand Parade start beneath Table Mountain, riders head south via Nelson Mandela Boulevard, Hospital Bend, and Edinburgh Drive (Wynberg Hill — a short, sharp early climb). Through the Southern Suburbs to the Indian Ocean coast at Muizenberg. Large pelotons form in the seeded groups. Flat to rolling urban roads.

segments.name: False Bay Coast: Muizenberg to Scarborough
segments.description: The Indian Ocean coast. Exposed, potentially windy, and the first real sense of the Peninsula's wild beauty. Through the charming seaside towns of Kalk Bay, Simon's Town, and Fish Hoek. Cross-winds off False Bay can be fierce. The road winds along the coast with the mountains of the Peninsula rising to the right. Past Kommetjie to Scarborough at the Peninsula's southern edge.

segments.name: Chapman's Peak Drive
segments.description: The moment. A cliff road carved into the mountainside 200 meters above the Atlantic Ocean, with 114 curves. The climb begins with 'Little Chappies' — short, steep, with enthusiastic spectator support at its base. Then the main ascent: approximately 5 km averaging 3-5% gradient, with the ocean dropping away below. On a clear day with no wind, this is one of the most beautiful cycling experiences on Earth. In a Southeast gale, it is a battle. The descent into Hout Bay is fast and sweeping with ocean and harbor views.

segments.name: Suikerbossie Hill
segments.description: The sting in the tail at km 95. From the Hout Bay roundabout, 2.8 km averaging 4.4% gradient. The final kilometre-and-a-half is purgatory — sustained climbing when legs are spent and minds are drifting toward the finish. It is the same for everyone. The infamous final climb of the Cape Town Cycle Tour.

segments.name: Atlantic Seaboard to Finish
segments.description: The reward. Camps Bay with its palm-lined promenade and white sand beach. Clifton. Sea Point. The Twelve Apostles mountain range to the right, the Atlantic Ocean to the left, Table Mountain behind. The finish at Green Point Cricket Fields. This stretch is either a victory lap on calm days or a final headwind battle when the Southeast blows.

key_features: Chapman's Peak Drive: 9 km cliff road with 114 curves, 200m above the Atlantic — the defining feature of the event
Two-ocean route: Indian Ocean (False Bay) and Atlantic Ocean in a single ride
Table Mountain as the backdrop at start and finish
Suikerbossie Hill: the cruel final climb at km 95 that catches tired riders
Fully or mostly closed roads for the entire 109 km circuit
course_description.strategy: The Cape Town Cycle Tour rewards disciplined pacing and wind awareness over raw power. Start conservatively in the Southern Suburbs — the temptation to hammer in a fast group is strong but the wind on False Bay will collect debts. Save matches for Chapman's Peak: the climb is not steep but the wind exposure can double the effort. Descend Chapman's carefully — 114 curves on a cliff road demand attention. Eat and drink steadily through the refreshment stations because the final 25 km (Suikerbossie + Atlantic Seaboard) can be brutal in a headwind. If the wind is calm, ride tempo and enjoy. If the Cape Doctor is howling, get in a group and stay there.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved coastal roads circling the Cape Peninsula
guide_variables.race_weather: Late summer: 15-27C (59-81F), mostly dry and sunny. Southeast 'Cape Doctor' wind is the primary hazard — gusts can exceed 100 km/h in extreme years.
race_challenges: Southeast wind: gusts of 40-100 km/h can dominate the entire ride and have caused event cancellations
Chapman's Peak climb: 9 km of cliff-road climbing — beautiful but exposed to wind
Sun exposure: 6+ hours of intense Southern Hemisphere UV with limited shade
Suikerbossie Hill at km 95: 2.8 km at 4.4% on spent legs

non_negotiables.requirement: Group riding and crosswind skills — confident in a peloton of 200+ riders in variable wind
non_negotiables.why: 28,000-35,000 riders on the road means dense groups, especially in the first 30 km. The Southeast wind creates dangerous crosswind echelons. You must be comfortable riding in tight packs and reacting to sudden gusts.

non_negotiables.requirement: Wind riding experience — ability to sustain power in headwinds and crosswinds for 3+ hours
non_negotiables.why: The Cape Doctor can turn the entire second half of the course into a headwind battle. Riders who only train in sheltered conditions crack when the wind hits on the exposed Peninsula sections.

non_negotiables.requirement: Heat and hydration strategy tested in warm conditions
non_negotiables.why: March in Cape Town can hit 27C+ in sheltered sections. Six hours in Southern Hemisphere sun with limited shade requires a proven hydration and sun protection plan. Dehydration on the Atlantic Seaboard with 15 km to go is common.

videos.title: Cape Town Cycle Tour 2025 – Full 4K Ride (Preview of the 2026 UCI Gran Fondo Qualifier Course)
videos.description:
The Cape Town Cycle Tour becomes a UCI Gran Fondo World Championship qualifier in 2026. This full 4K onboard ride from 2025 (Group 1C) is one of the closest real-world course previews for anyone training to qualify next year.

This video covers the entire 2025 Cape Town Cycle Tour from inside Group 1C, including every climb, descent, bunch split, and pacing decision from my third attempt at breaking the 3-hour barrier. If you’re preparing for the 2026 UCI qualifier edition, this is one of the most accurate representations of race speed and dynamics available online.

🚴 Bike Setup

• 2023 Giant Propel
• Continental GP5000 S TR 28 mm tubeless @ 65 psi
• MBPC race kit
• Race weight ~80–81 kg
• Full 4K action camera footage

⛰️ Key CTCT Segments Covered

• Edinburgh Drive – early selection
• Smitswinkel – power-to-weight test
• Chapman’s Peak – fatigue management
• Suikerbossie – decisive climb
• Green Point run-in – high-speed finish

These same sections will be critical qualification points when CTCT becomes a UCI Gran Fondo event in 2026.

Although this 2025 event was not a UCI qualifier, the pacing, bunch movement, weather, and race flow provide an excellent reference for:
• international riders visiting Cape Town in 2026
• age-group athletes aiming to qualify
• cyclists looking for realistic race speeds
• Sub-3 attempts and pacing ideas
• anyone wanting an accurate course preview

🙏 Thank You

Thanks to everyone who said hello—many of you appear in the footage! Huge thanks to fellow riders for a fast, safe, and enjoyable race. This was my third tour and one of the most memorable.
2025 time: 3:10:55
Overall position: 1602 / 17,932
Age group (55–59): 158 / 1,872
Group 1C: 151 / 247

Special mentions:
• Andrew Woodburn (2-up partner through Muizenberg)
• Aidan Whittles—blistering Sub-3 debut
• My PE riding friends for an amazing stay
• Ian for carrying camera gear
• My wife, family, and Matt Bottrill Performance Coaching for getting me to the start line in top shape

The Cape Town Cycle Tour Trust delivered another world-class event—thank you to everyone involved.

videos.title: Cape Town Cycle Tour : Predictions, Taper & Nutrition, Classics Season has arrived Ep8 | Breakaway
videos.description:
Cape Town Cycle Tour race week is its own sport: tapering without going stale, fueling without doing anything “new,” and staying calm when the roads (and the vibes) get hectic.

In this episode of The Breakaway Show, David and Sarah break down what the final week should actually look like—from elite-level readiness to “punter” practicality. You’ll hear how to think about rest days vs openers, why you shouldn’t chase “race weight” in taper week, and how to keep your energy stable when work stress and travel are peaking.

Then we go deep on race-day fueling: carb targets, mixing strategies, and the real-world way people use caffeine (and why timing matters). Sarah explains caffeine’s effect on perceived exertion, when it helps, and when it can backfire—especially for anxious riders.

We also hit the most important topic: safety. Wind, descents, bunch dynamics, marshal awareness, and the simple habits that keep everyone getting home in one piece.

Finally, we pivot to the weekend’s racing: the classics season opening, Demi Vollering’s statement ride, Paul Seixas’ breakout win, and a quick crash course on monuments. We wrap with Tuesday segment chaos (Engine-to-Engine vs Chappies) and a tech rabbit hole on Challenge’s new Stagioni tire and why tires might be the most underrated upgrade you can make.

Subscribe for weekly cycling + running talk, tech rabbit holes, and local stories that deserve the spotlight.

videos.title: Cape Town Cycle Tour 2024 Full Group 1A Footage
videos.description:
Full race footage in 4K with front and rear camera views of starting group 1A, with my group merging with the front of group 1B through to the finish. 
If you were in the rear of group 1A or in group 1B and feature in the footage let me know in the comments at what time and in what kit
Thank you to all fellow participants for fast but fun and safe riding, it made my second tour most memorable. I hope to be back in 2025 to improve on my 2024 time of 3 hours 14 minutes 5 seconds and get closer to that 3 hour mark
I finished in 1113th place out of 17924 finishers and in 82th place out of 1930 in my age category 55-59

Special shout out to the super quick 1B rider in the yellow Sci Con kit - sorry I didn't have the legs to stay with you but enjoyed the short lived 2 up none the less. Also to Brent Pheiffer of Wayne Pheiffer Cycles for that magic helping hand over Smitswinkel - we seemed to stay close from then on to the finish. And to my PE cycling friends for making my stay memorable thank you Avron, Brian and Ian and the rest of the crew for all the race advice and support
Of course thank you to my Coach Bob Tobin and Matt Bottrill of Matt Bottrill Performance Coaching for getting myself and the bike to the start line in the best possible condition - Matt, your aero handlebars worked a treat

The Cape Town Cycle Tour Trust put on another world class event  - congratulations and thank you to them for all the hard work involved to ensure it all ran smoothly

rider_intel.search_text: The Cape Town Cycle Tour follows a familiar peninsula route starting on the Blue Route to Muizenberg, then hitting the coastline where wind can be a major factor. Riders snake through Fishoek and Simonstown before the first real challenge at Smith ville climb. The route continues through Misty Cliffs where temperatures drop 5-6 degrees, then up Chapman's Peak described as the most beautiful road in the world. The course finishes through Sea Point after going over Suikerbossie. Race week sees Cape Town buzzing with energy as riders arrive from Johannesburg and across South Africa. Training approaches vary widely during taper week, with some riders doing their final ride days before the event while others continue high-intensity intervals. The event maintains its original purpose of getting more people cycling for commuting, not just racing, and serves as a major charity fundraising platform.
